title: Neuropharmacological Studies on Ibogaine, an Indole Alkaloid with Central-Stimulant Properties
authors: Schneider, J. A.; Sigg, E. B.
key_findings: Ibogaine produces EEG arousal via ascending reticular formation; effect blocked by atropine implicating cholinergic mechanism; paradoxical anticonvulsant properties despite central stimulation
journal: Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences
aliases: Schneider 1957; Schneider Sigg Ibogaine Neuropharmacology; CIBA Ibogaine Reticular Formation

title: Psychotherapeutic Possibilities of New Fantasy-Enhancing Drugs
authors: Naranjo, Claudio
key_findings: FOUNDATIONAL: First systematic phenomenological description of ibogaine/harmaline as 'oneirophrenics' - dream-like fantasy enhancement without psychosis. Coined term for ibogaine's unique mental state distinct from psychotomimetics.

title: A Comparative Study on the Toxicity of Ibogaine and Serotonin
authors: Dhahir, Hashim Ismail
tags: topic/toxicity; topic/cardiac; topic/pharmacokinetics; topic/adverse-event; topic/serotonin; topic/mechanism; topic/receptor; topic/alcohol; method/preclinical
key_findings: Ibogaine LD₅₀ 145 mg/kg rat i.p.; peaks liver/kidney 1h, cleared 12h; 15% excreted as indolic metabolite (not 5-HIAA); vagal-mediated hypotension/bradycardia (atropine-reversible); ethanol 2g/kg potentiates toxicity 1.4×
dosing_range: Acute LD₅₀: 145 mg/kg i.p. (rat), 175 mg/kg i.p. (mouse), 327 mg/kg i.g. (rat). Chronic: 10–50 mg/kg i.p. (30 days), 40 mg/kg i.p. (12 days). Cardiovascular: 2–32 mg/kg i.v.
aliases: Dhahir 1971; Ibogaine Serotonin Toxicity Comparison; Ibogaine PhD Dissertation

title: Ibogaine and the inhibition of morphine self-administration: aftereffects of ibogaine on morphine self-administration in rats
authors: Glick, Stanley D.; Rossman, Kenner; Steindorf, Samara; Maisonneuve, Isabelle M.; Carlson, John N.
key_findings: Ibogaine (10-80 mg/kg i.p.) dose-dependently reduced morphine self-administration in rats acutely and ≥1 day post-dose; some rats showed persistent decreases lasting weeks. Aftereffect not attributable to conditioned aversion or motor impairment.

title: Interactions between ibogaine, a potential anti-addictive agent, and morphine: an in vivo microdialysis study
authors: Maisonneuve, I. M.; Keller, R. W. Jr.; Glick, S. D.
key_findings: First in vivo microdialysis of ibogaine–morphine DA interactions. Ibogaine decreased striatal DA, increased prefrontal DA, and blocked morphine-induced DA rise 19 h post-dose across three brain regions.

title: Mechanisms of action of ibogaine and harmaline congeners based on radioligand binding studies
authors: Darlene C. Deecher; Milton Teitler; David M. Soderlund; William G. Bornmann; Martin E. Kuehne; Stanley D. Glick
tags: topic/receptor; topic/opioid; topic/analogues; mechanism/kappa-opioid; mechanism/ion-channel; method/in-vitro
key_findings: Ibogaine binds κ-opioid receptors (Ki=2.08 μM) but not μ or δ via [³H]carfentanil; binding is reversible. Ibogaine and harmaline congeners interact with voltage-dependent sodium channels (μM range). No GABA interaction found.

title: Effects of ibogaine on naloxone-precipitated withdrawal in morphine-dependent mice
authors: Francés, Bernard; Gout, R.; Cros, J.; Zajac, Jean-Marie
key_findings: Ibogaine (30 mg/kg IP) failed to attenuate morphine withdrawal in mice and significantly potentiated naloxone-precipitated jumping (~9-fold), suggesting amplification of opioid-mediated responses rather than anti-withdrawal activity.
aliases: Francés 1992; Ibogaine morphine withdrawal mice potentiation

title: Effects of Ibogaine on Acute Signs of Morphine Withdrawal in Rats: Independence from Tremor
authors: Glick, Stanley D.; Rossman, Karen; Rao, N. C.; Maisonneuve, Isabelle M.; Carlson, J. Neil
key_findings: Ibogaine (40-80 mg/kg IP) significantly reduced 4 of 7 morphine withdrawal signs in rats; effects persisted 4 hr post-dose when tremors had fully dissipated, confirming independence from motor confounds.
dosing_range: 20, 40 and 80 mg/kg IP (ibogaine HCl in saline, 1–4 ml/kg)
aliases: Glick 1992; Ibogaine morphine withdrawal tremor independence; Glick1992_Morphine_Withdrawal_Rats

title: Ibogaine Antagonizes Cocaine-Induced Locomotor Stimulation in Mice
authors: Sershen, Henry; Hashim, Audrey; Harsing, Laszlo; Lajtha, Abel
key_findings: Ibogaine (40 mg/kg) reduces cocaine-induced locomotor stimulation in mice for ≥9 days. Selective for cocaine (not amphetamine). Transiently increases DA turnover (DOPAC+HVA/DA). Weak DAT affinity (IC₅₀ 1500 nM vs cocaine 120 nM).

title: Inhibitory effects of ibogaine on cocaine self-administration in rats
authors: Cappendijk, Susanne L.T.; Dzoljic, Michailo R.
key_findings: Single ibogaine dose (40 mg/kg i.p.) reduced rat cocaine self-administration for >48h; weekly repeated dosing produced cumulative, more sustained inhibition suggesting long-lasting active metabolite(s)

title: Degeneration of Purkinje Cells in Parasagittal Zones of the Cerebellar Vermis after Treatment with Ibogaine or Harmaline
authors: O'Hearn, Elizabeth; Molliver, Mark E.
key_findings: Ibogaine (100 mg/kg) and harmaline cause selective Purkinje cell degeneration in parasagittal zones of rat cerebellar vermis via proposed olivocerebellar excitotoxic mechanism. Doses 4× proposed human dose.
aliases: O'Hearn 1993; Purkinje Cell Degeneration Ibogaine; Cerebellar Neurotoxicity Ibogaine

title: Effects of iboga alkaloids on morphine and cocaine self-administration in rats: relationship to tremorigenic effects and to effects on dopamine release in nucleus accumbens and striatum
authors: Glick, Stanley D.; Kuehne, Martin E.; Raucci, Joseph; Wilson, Thomas E.; Larson, David; Keller, Robert W. Jr.; Carlson, J. Neil
key_findings: R-enantiomers of ibogamine/coronaridine reduced morphine & cocaine self-admin without tremor; R-isomers decreased DA in NAC/STR while S-isomers had no effect. Anti-addictive and tremorigenic effects dissociable.

title: The putative anti-addictive drug ibogaine is a competitive inhibitor of [3H]MK-801 binding to the NMDA receptor complex
authors: Popik, Piotr; Layer, Richard T.; Skolnick, Phil
key_findings: Ibogaine competitively inhibits [³H]MK-801 binding at NMDA receptor ion channels (Kᵢ = 1.01 ± 0.1 μM, nH = 1.2). No effect on AMPA, kainate, or metabotropic glutamate receptors up to 100 μM.
aliases: Popik 1994; Ibogaine NMDA MK-801 competitive binding

title: Ibogaine Reduces Preference for Cocaine Consumption in C57BL/6By Mice
authors: Sershen, Henry; Hashim, Audrey; Lajtha, Abel
key_findings: Ibogaine (2×40 mg/kg IP) reduced cocaine preference from 71% to 41% and intake by 38% for ≥5 days in C57BL/6By mice. Attenuated cocaine-induced locomotor sensitisation independently of brain cocaine levels.
dosing_range: 2×40 mg/kg IP ibogaine HCl (6 h apart); cocaine 200–400 mg/l oral, 25 mg/kg SC challenge

title: A Preliminary Investigation of Ibogaine: Case Reports and Recommendations for Further Study
authors: Sheppard, Simon G.
key_findings: First published case series (N=7): ibogaine 700–1800 mg (11.7–25 mg/kg) abolished opiate withdrawal in all subjects; 3/7 remained drug-free ≥14 weeks; higher doses correlated with better outcomes.
aliases: Sheppard 1994; First ibogaine case series; Amsterdam ibogaine case reports; Hirez ibogaine study

title: Ibogaine and Its Congeners Are σ Receptor-Selective Ligands with Moderate Affinity
authors: Bowen, Wayne D.; Vilner, Bertold J.; Williams, Wanda; Bertha, Craig M.; Kuehne, Martin E.; Jacobson, Arthur E.
key_findings: Ibogaine binds σ₂ receptors (Kᵢ = 201 nM) with 43-fold selectivity over σ₁ — highest affinity target known at time. O-desmethylibogaine loses σ₂ affinity (Kᵢ = 5226 nM), suggesting demethylation terminates σ-mediated effects.

title: Effects of ibogaine on responding maintained by food, cocaine and heroin reinforcement in rats
authors: Dworkin, Steven I.; Gleeson, Suzanne; Meloni, Domenico; Koves, Timothy R.; Martin, Thomas J.
key_findings: Ibogaine (80 mg/kg IP) suppressed food, cocaine, and heroin self-administration in rats; heroin responding most sensitive. Effects not drug-specific — motor impairment may contribute.
dosing_range: 40 and 80 mg/kg IP (ibogaine in water, 10 mg/ml)
aliases: Dworkin 1995; Ibogaine Food Cocaine Heroin Self-Administration Rats

title: Identification and Quantitation of Ibogaine and an o-Demethylated Metabolite in Brain and Biological Fluids Using Gas Chromatography-Mass Spectrometry
authors: Hearn, William L.; Pablo, John; Hime, George W.; Mash, Deborah C.
key_findings: First validated GC-MS method for ibogaine and noribogaine (12-OH-ibogamine) in biological fluids/brain. LOD 5ng/mL. Identified noribogaine as major metabolite. Foundation for pharmacokinetic research.

title: Identification of a Primary Metabolite of Ibogaine That Targets Serotonin Transporters and Elevates Serotonin
authors: Mash, Deborah C.; Staley, Julie K.; Baumann, Michael H.; Rothman, Richard B.; Hearn, W. Lee
key_findings: First identification of noribogaine (12-hydroxyibogamine) as ibogaine's primary metabolite; Kᵢ 40.7 nM at SERT (10-fold > ibogaine); 3-fold 5-HT elevation in nucleus accumbens; long half-life with 508–706 ng/ml at 24h post-dose

title: Radioligand-binding study of noribogaine, a likely metabolite of ibogaine
authors: Pearl, Sandra M.; Herrick-Davis, Katherine; Teitler, Milton; Glick, Stanley D.
key_findings: Noribogaine shows 3-4× higher affinity than ibogaine at κ (Kᵢ=0.96 μM vs 3.77 μM) and μ (Kᵢ=2.66 μM vs 11.04 μM) opioid receptors, and unlike ibogaine binds δ receptors (Kᵢ=24.72 μM)

title: 100 Years of Ibogaine: Neurochemical and Pharmacological Actions of a Putative Anti-addictive Drug
authors: Popik, Piotr; Layer, Richard T.; Skolnick, Phil
key_findings: LANDMARK REVIEW: Comprehensive 100-year overview of ibogaine pharmacology from NIH team. Covers NMDA antagonism, sigma receptor binding, dopamine modulation. Essential reference for mechanism understanding.
aliases: Popik 1995; Popik; 100 Years of Ibogaine; Popik1995_100Years_Ibogaine_COMPLETE; Popik1995_100_Years_Ibogaine

title: Attenuation of Alcohol Intake by Ibogaine in Three Strains of Alcohol-Preferring Rats
authors: Rezvani, Amir H.; Overstreet, David H.; Lee, Yue-Wei
key_findings: Ibogaine IP/IG (not SC) dose-dependently reduced alcohol intake in P, FH, AA rats; 60 mg/kg IP: 51–65% reduction; 5-day IG dosing showed no tolerance; no effect on blood alcohol; suggests hepatic metabolic activation required

title: Receptor binding profile suggests multiple mechanisms of action are responsible for ibogaine's putative anti-addictive activity
authors: Sweetnam, P. M.; Lancaster, J.; Snowman, Adele; Collins, J. L.; Perschke, S.; Bauer, C.; Ferkany, J.
key_findings: Ibogaine binds 14 receptors at 1–100 μM: dopamine uptake IC₅₀ 3.5 μM, NMDA/MK-801 5.6 μM, 5-HT₃ 3.9 μM, 5-HT₂ 4.8 μM, κ-opioid 16 μM; no nanomolar affinity at any site.

title: Neurochemical and Behavioural Interactions Between Ibogaine and Nicotine in the Rat
authors: Benwell, Maureen E.M.; Holtom, Pamela E.; Moran, Robert J.; Balfour, David J.K.
key_findings: Ibogaine (40 mg/kg IP) attenuated nicotine-induced NAc dopamine overflow 22h post-treatment but not locomotion; produced long-lasting anxiogenesis and mPFC neurochemical changes persisting ≥7 days
dosing_range: 40 mg/kg IP (ibogaine in 25% ethanol/sterile water, 1.0 ml/kg)

title: Ibogaine Block of the NMDA Receptor: In Vitro and In Vivo Studies
authors: Chen, Kang; Kokate, Tushar G.; Donevan, Sean D.; Carroll, F. Ivy; Rogawski, Michael A.
key_findings: Ibogaine is an open channel NMDA receptor antagonist (IC₅₀ 3.1 µM, -60 mV). Block is use- and voltage-dependent, occluded by Mg²⁺. In vivo MES protection ED₅₀ 31 mg/kg i.p.; LD₅₀ 213 mg/kg in mice.
aliases: Chen 1996; Ibogaine NMDA open channel block

title: 18-Methoxycoronaridine, a non-toxic iboga alkaloid congener: effects on morphine and cocaine self-administration and on mesolimbic dopamine release in rats
authors: Glick, Stanley D.; Maisonneuve, Isabelle M.; Szumlinski, Karen K.
key_findings: BREAKTHROUGH: First demonstration that 18-MC provides equivalent anti-addictive efficacy to ibogaine (decreases morphine/cocaine self-admin) without tremors or cerebellar toxicity. Safer ibogaine analogue.

title: Tissue Distribution of Ibogaine After Intraperitoneal and Subcutaneous Administration
authors: Hough, Lindsay B.; Pearl, Sandra M.; Glick, Stanley D.
key_findings: Ibogaine accumulates 100-fold in fat vs plasma after 40 mg/kg in rats; sc route yields higher levels than ip (first-pass effect); adipose reservoir may explain prolonged duration of action

title: Structurally modified ibogaine analogs exhibit differing affinities for NMDA receptors
authors: Layer, Richard T.; Skolnick, Phil; Bertha, Craig M.; Bandarage, Upul K.; Kuehne, Martin E.; Popik, Piotr
key_findings: Ibogaine most potent NMDA antagonist in series (Kᵢ = 1.1 μM); O-desmethylibogaine ~5× weaker. Only ibogaine attenuated naloxone-precipitated withdrawal in mice, supporting NMDA-mediated anti-addictive mechanism.

title: Ibogaine, Trauma, and Abreaction: The Treatment of Chemical Dependence
authors: Lotsof, Howard S.; Smith, C. A.; Bastiaans, J.
key_findings: FOUNDATIONAL CLINICAL: First documentation of ibogaine-induced abreaction and trauma processing. Ibogaine eliminates withdrawal while enabling non-emotional trauma access - key psychological mechanism for addiction treatment.
dosing_range: 10 mg/kg (PK study) and 20 mg/kg (therapeutic) oral — Case 2 only; doses not specified for Cases 1 and 3

title: Ibogaine neurotoxicity: a re-evaluation
authors: Molinari, H.H.; Maisonneuve, I.M.; Glick, S.D.
key_findings: 40 mg/kg ibogaine (anti-addictive dose) produced no cerebellar Purkinje cell degeneration in rats; only high doses (≥100 mg/kg) caused degeneration, confined to lobules 5-7 and simplex. Anti-addictive and neurotoxic properties are dissociable.

title: Acute and Chronic Administration of Ibogaine to the Rat Results in Astrogliosis That Is Not Confined to the Cerebellar Vermis
authors: O'Callaghan, James P.; Rogers, Tina S.; Rodman, Larry E.; Page, John G.
key_findings: Ibogaine causes dose-, sex-, region-dependent astrogliosis in rats. Chronic oral dosing: up to 215% GFAP increases in female hippocampus, brain stem, striatum; males unaffected. NF-68/beta-tubulin >2-fold in females. Gliosis beyond cerebellar vermis.
journal: Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences

title: Neuropharmacological characterization of local ibogaine effects on dopamine release
authors: Reid, M. S.; Hsu, K. Jr.; Souza, K. H.; Broderick, P. A.; Berger, S. P.
key_findings: BIPHASIC MECHANISM: Local ibogaine shows dose-dependent dual effects - low doses (10⁻⁶-10⁻⁴M) decrease dopamine via kappa receptors, high doses (10⁻³M) increase dopamine via DAT interaction.

title: Ibogaine Produces Neurodegeneration in Rat, but Not Mouse, Cerebellum: Neurohistological Biomarkers of Purkinje Cell Loss
authors: Scallet, A. C.; Ye, X.; Rountree, R.; Nony, P.; Ali, S. F.
key_findings: Ibogaine 100 mg/kg i.p. causes parasagittal Purkinje cell degeneration in rat cerebellum (silver stain, calbindin loss, GFAP astrocytosis) but no neurotoxicity in mice at same dose. H&E staining insufficient—risks false negatives.
journal: Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences

title: Pharmacological screen for activities of 12-hydroxyibogamine: a primary metabolite of the indole alkaloid ibogaine
authors: Staley, Julie K.; Ouyang, Qinjie; Pablo, John; Hearn, Wayne L.; Flynn, Dwight D.; Rothman, Richard B.; Rice, Kenner C.; Mash, Deborah C.
key_findings: Noribogaine 10× more potent than ibogaine at SERT (IC₅₀ 40.7 nM vs 590 nM). Both ~equipotent at DAT (~3-4 μM). Brain levels 1-17 μM post-oral dosing; metabolite rises 10-fold over 2h as parent declines.
aliases: Staley 1996; Noribogaine Receptor Binding Profile; 12-Hydroxyibogamine Pharmacological Screen

title: Time-dependent interactions between iboga agents and cocaine
authors: Maisonneuve, Isabelle M.; Visker, Kathleen E.; Mann, Gina L.; Bandarage, Upul K.; Kuehne, Martin E.; Glick, Stanley D.
key_findings: BIPHASIC TIMING: All iboga agents (ibogaine, noribogaine, 18-MC) inhibit cocaine hyperactivity at 1h but potentiate it at 19h post-administration. Explains divergent literature findings.

title: 18-Methoxycoronaridine attenuates nicotine-induced dopamine release and nicotine preferences in rats
authors: Glick, Stanley D.; Maisonneuve, Isabelle M.; Visker, Kathleen E.; Fritz, K. A.; Bandarage, Upul K.; Kuehne, Martin E.
key_findings: THERAPEUTIC EXPANSION: 18-MC extends to nicotine addiction - attenuates nicotine-induced NAc dopamine release (19h pretreatment) and abolishes nicotine preference 24h+. Reduces nicotine without affecting water intake.

title: Mechanisms of Antiaddictive Actions of Ibogaine
authors: Glick, Stanley D.; Maisonneuve, Isabelle M.
tags: topic/mechanism; topic/receptor; topic/dopamine; topic/opioid; topic/noribogaine; mechanism/kappa-opioid; mechanism/nmda-antagonism; mechanism/nicotinic-receptor; method/preclinical; method/review
key_findings: Ibogaine antiaddictive effects via κ-opioid + NMDA (opioids/stimulants), SERT (alcohol), nAChR antagonism (nicotine); σ₂ mediates neurotoxicity; 18-MC dissociates efficacy from toxicity.
journal: Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences
aliases: Glick 1998; Glick Maisonneuve 1998 NYAS; Mechanisms of antiaddictive actions ibogaine

title: Observations on Treatment with Ibogaine
authors: Luciano, Daniel
key_findings: Ibogaine 20-25 mg/kg caused transient vestibulocerebellar dysfunction (nystagmus, intention tremor, ataxia) resolving by 24h; no ECG or EEG abnormalities; no withdrawal/craving at 24h
dosing_range: 1 mg/kg test dose (day 1); 20–25 mg/kg ibogaine HCl full dose (day 2)

title: Ibogaine acts at the nicotinic acetylcholine receptor cation channel to inhibit catecholamine release
authors: Mah, S. J.
key_findings: MECHANISM: Low-dose ibogaine (10µM) selectively inhibits nicotinic receptor-mediated catecholamine release without affecting K+ or veratridine-evoked release. Not kappa-opioid mediated. Concentration-dependent reversibility.

title: Medication Development of Ibogaine as a Pharmacotherapy for Drug Dependence
authors: Mash, Deborah C.; Kovera, Craig A.; Buck, Billy E.; Norenberg, Michael D.; Shapshak, Paul; Hearn, W. Lee; Sanchez-Ramos, Juan
key_findings: First human PK data: ibogaine peaks ~4 hr (600–1250 ng/ml), noribogaine persists >24 hr (~800 ng/ml). Identified noribogaine as active metabolite with μ-opioid partial agonist activity (Ki ~200 nM). 30 subjects, no significant AEs at 500–800 mg.
journal: Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences
aliases: Mash 1998; Ibogaine Medication Development Review; Noribogaine Discovery Paper

title: Cytochrome P4502D6 Catalyzes the O-Demethylation of the Psychoactive Alkaloid Ibogaine to 12-Hydroxyibogamine
authors: Obach, R. Scott; Pablo, John; Mash, Deborah C.
key_findings: CYP2D6 is the primary enzyme catalysing ibogaine O-demethylation to noribogaine (KMapp 1.1 µM, IC₅₀ quinidine 0.2 µM, r²=0.711 with bufuralol 1'-hydroxylase). Poor metabolisers lack this activity.

title: Noribogaine stimulates naloxone-sensitive [³⁵S]GTPγS binding
authors: Pablo, John P.; Mash, Deborah C.
key_findings: Noribogaine is a full μ-opioid agonist (EC₅₀ 324 nM, 170% maximal stimulation) comparable to DAMGO and morphine; ibogaine has negligible intrinsic activity. Identical G-protein population activated.

title: Ibogaine
authors: Witter, Simon
key_findings: JOURNALISM: Comprehensive 1998 landscape review documenting Lotsof's 1962 discovery, treatment providers (Lotsof/Panama, Mash/St Kitts, Taub/Costa Rica), costs ($2,200-$20,000), and first-person experience reports.

title: Treatment of Acute Opioid Withdrawal with Ibogaine
authors: Alper, Kenneth R.; Lotsof, Howard S.; Frenken, Geerte M. N.; Luciano, Daniel J.; Bastiaans, Jan
key_findings: LANDMARK CASE SERIES: n=33 opioid-dependent patients (avg 0.64g heroin/day IV). 76% (25/33) achieved complete withdrawal resolution within 24h sustained 72h. One fatality (possible surreptitious heroin use).

title: (±)-18-Methoxycoronaridine: A Novel Iboga Alkaloid Congener Having Potential Anti-Addictive Efficacy
authors: Glick, Stanley D.; Maisonneuve, Isabelle M.; Hough, Lindsay B.; Kuehne, Martin E.; Bandarage, Upul K.
tags: topic/18-mc; topic/analogues; topic/harm-reduction; topic/receptor; topic/dopamine; mechanism/nicotinic-receptor; method/review
key_findings: DEFINITIVE 18-MC REVIEW: Synthesises preclinical evidence. Zero tremors/cerebellar toxicity at 100mg/kg (vs ibogaine Purkinje damage). Equivalent anti-addictive efficacy across morphine, cocaine, alcohol, nicotine.

title: Attenuation of the reinforcing efficacy of morphine by 18-methoxycoronaridine
authors: Maisonneuve, Isabelle M.; Glick, Stanley D.
key_findings: 18-MC (40 mg/kg, 19 h pre) inhibits morphine-induced NAc dopamine release while enhancing metabolites. Downward shift in morphine dose–response — reduces reinforcing efficacy without altering potency.

title: Ibogaine in Acute Opioid Withdrawal: An Open Label Case Series
authors: Alper, Kenneth R.; Lotsof, Howard S.; Frenken, Geerte M. N.; Luciano, Daniel J.; Bastiaans, Jan
key_findings: NYAS proceedings of Alper1999 case series: 33 opioid-dependent patients, 76% (25/33) complete withdrawal resolution within 24h sustained 72h. One fatality. Discusses 18-MC as potential lower-psychoactivity congener.
journal: Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences
aliases: Alper 2000; Alper Ibogaine Acute Opioid Withdrawal 2000; Alper NYAS 2000

title: Application of Electrophysiological Method to Study Interactions between Ibogaine and Cocaine
authors: Binienda, Zbigniew; Beaudoin, Michael A.; Thorn, Brett T.; Sadovova, Natalya; Skinner, Robert D.; Slikker, William Jr.; Ali, Syed F.
key_findings: ELECTROPHYSIOLOGY: IBO pretreatment (50mg/kg, 1h prior) dramatically altered cocaine ECoG response - increased delta/theta/alpha1 power vs cocaine alone. DA levels decreased further with IBO/COC combination. Suggests decreased cocaine response.
dosing_range: 50 mg/kg ibogaine (rat i.p.); 20 mg/kg cocaine (rat i.p.)
journal: Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences

title: 18-Methoxycoronaridine (18-MC) and Ibogaine: Comparison of Antiaddictive Efficacy, Toxicity, and Mechanisms of Action
authors: Glick, Stanley D.; Maisonneuve, Isabelle M.; Szumlinski, Karen K.
key_findings: 18-MC vs ibogaine comparison: equal anti-addictive efficacy for morphine, cocaine, alcohol, nicotine. 18-MC lacks tremor, cerebellar damage, bradycardia. Lower NMDA/sigma-2 affinity. Greater therapeutic index than ibogaine.

title: Development of Novel Medications for Drug Addiction: The Legacy of an African Shrub
authors: Glick, Stanley D.; Maisonneuve, Isabelle M.
tags: topic/18-mc; topic/analogues; topic/mechanism; topic/dopamine; topic/receptor; method/review
key_findings: 18-MC matches ibogaine anti-addictive efficacy across morphine, cocaine, alcohol, nicotine without tremor or bradycardia. Downward shift in dose-response suggests reduced reinforcing efficacy.
journal: Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences
aliases: Glick 2000 Legacy; Glick Maisonneuve 2000 NYAS; Legacy of an African Shrub

title: Neurologic, Electroencephalographic and General Medical Observations in Subjects Administered Ibogaine
authors: Luciano, Daniel J.; Della Sera, Edgardo A.; Jethmal, Ezequiel G.
tags: topic/adverse-event; topic/withdrawal; topic/opioid; topic/motor; topic/stimulant; method/case-series
key_findings: First human EEG study during ibogaine — all recordings normal. Universal transient cerebellar ataxia resolved by 24h. Movement-induced vomiting (3/5). No withdrawal symptoms at 24h; 2 subjects symptom-free at 1 week.
contraindications: Seizure disorder; Hypertension; Cardiac disease; Hepatic disease; Renal disease; DSM-4 Axis 1 diagnoses
dosing_range: 1 mg/kg test dose + 0.5 mg/kg sensitivity test + ~25 mg/kg treatment dose oral (ibogaine HCl); additional 5 mg/kg oral if early vomiting; rectal re-administration used in 1 subject with persistent vomiting

title: Ibogaine: Complex Pharmacokinetics, Concerns for Safety, and Preliminary Efficacy Measures
authors: Mash, Deborah C.; Kovera, Craig A.; Pablo, Javier; Tyndale, Rolando F.; Ervin, Frank R.; Williams, Irwin C.; Singleton, Edward F.; Mayor, Martin
key_findings: FOUNDATIONAL PK STUDY: n=33 Phase I trials establishing ibogaine→noribogaine metabolism. Ibogaine T1/2 4-7h, noribogaine accumulates with long elimination. Cardiovascular effects including bradycardia and QT prolongation observed.
journal: Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences

title: Pharmacological comparison of the effect of ibogaine and 18-methoxycoronaridine on isolated smooth muscle from the rat and guinea-pig
authors: Mundey, M. K.; Blaylock, N. A.; Mason, R.; Glick, Stanley D.; Maisonneuve, Isabelle M.; Wilson, V. G.
key_findings: PERIPHERAL: No evidence for µ-opioid receptor activity - naloxone-insensitive inhibition of ileum contractions. Novel finding: both agents enhance purinergic contractions 3-5 fold. Ibogaine enhances portal vein contractions; 18-MC does not.

title: A Dose-Response Study of Ibogaine-Induced Neuropathology in the Rat Cerebellum
authors: Xu, Zengjun; Chang, Louis W.; Slikker, William Jr.; Ali, Syed F.; Rountree, Robert L.; Scallet, Andrew C.
key_findings: Ibogaine ip NOAEL 25 mg/kg in female rats. Purkinje cell degeneration dose-dependent: 0/6 at 25, 2/6 at 50, 6/6 at 75–100 mg/kg. GFAP most sensitive biomarker.
contraindications: Pre-existing cerebellar pathology

title: A Contemporary History of Ibogaine in the United States and Europe
authors: Alper, Kenneth R.; Beal, Dana; Kaplan, Charles D.
key_findings: Comprehensive narrative of ibogaine treatment scenes in US and Europe 1962-2001, documenting Lotsof's discovery, advocacy movements, and regulatory landscape
aliases: Alper 2001 History; Contemporary History of Ibogaine

title: Ibogaine: A Review
authors: Alper, Kenneth R.
tags: topic/mechanism; topic/receptor; topic/pharmacokinetics; topic/noribogaine; topic/cardiac; topic/toxicity; topic/opioid; topic/18-mc; topic/history; mechanism/nmda-antagonism; mechanism/sigma-receptor; mechanism/nicotinic-receptor; mechanism/sert-inhibition; mechanism/kappa-opioid; method/review; method/preclinical
key_findings: Comprehensive ibogaine pharmacology synthesis: NMDA, opioid, 5-HT, nicotinic interactions. Novel mechanism distinct from existing treatments. 10 μM brain levels validate in vitro activities.
contraindications: Pre-existing cardiac disease; Concomitant opioid use

title: In Vivo Neurobiological Effects of Ibogaine and Its O-Desmethyl Metabolite, 12-Hydroxyibogamine (Noribogaine), in Rats
authors: Baumann, Michael H.; Rothman, Richard B.; Pablo, John P.; Mash, Deborah C.
key_findings: Noribogaine is ~10× more potent than ibogaine as indirect 5-HT agonist via SERT inhibition. Ibogaine causes tremors/ataxia; noribogaine does not. Neither drug alters accumbens DA. Noribogaine may be safer alternative.
journal: Journal of Pharmacology and Experimental Therapeutics

title: Returning to the Path: The Use of Iboga[ine] in an Equatorial African Ritual Context and the Binding of Time, Space, and Social Relationships
authors: Fernandez, James W.; Fernandez, Renate L.
key_findings: Reframes Bwiti iboga use as 'time and space binding' against colonial anomie; compares initiation with INTASH therapy; argues collectivist aftercare model outperforms Western individualism
aliases: Fernandez 2001; Fernandez & Fernandez 2001; Returning to the Path

title: From the Roots Up: Ibogaine and Addict Self-Help
authors: Frenken, Geerte
tags: topic/history; topic/harm-reduction; topic/opioid; topic/withdrawal; topic/traditional-use; topic/policy; method/qualitative
key_findings: Dutch addict self-help movement (INTASH) treated 8 opiate-dependent subjects with ibogaine 20 mg/kg; 7/8 achieved 6-month abstinence with complete acute withdrawal resolution
dosing_range: 20 mg/kg ibogaine HCl oral (capsules, single dose) for addiction interruption; 10 mg/kg for initiatory sessions
aliases: Frenken 2001; INTASH Self-Help Chapter; From the Roots Up

title: Mechanisms of Action of Ibogaine: Relevance to Putative Therapeutic Effects and Development of a Safer Iboga Alkaloid Congener
authors: Glick, Stanley D.; Maisonneuve, Isabelle M.; Szumlinski, Karen K.
tags: topic/mechanism; topic/receptor; topic/dopamine; topic/opioid; topic/noribogaine; topic/18-mc; topic/toxicity; mechanism/kappa-opioid; mechanism/nmda-antagonism; method/preclinical; method/review
key_findings: Comprehensive receptor screen of ibogaine, noribogaine, and 18-MC across 23 binding sites; 18-MC mimics antiaddictive efficacy without tremor, cerebellar toxicity, or cardiac effects; both reverse sensitised dopamine responses.
aliases: Glick 2001; Glick Maisonneuve Szumlinski 2001; Mechanisms of action ibogaine 18-MC Alkaloids

title: Case Studies of Ibogaine Treatment: Implications for Patient Management Strategies
authors: Lotsof, Howard S.; Alexander, Norma E.
tags: topic/opioid; topic/withdrawal; topic/phenomenology; topic/protocol; topic/history; topic/efficacy; topic/harm-reduction; method/case-series
key_findings: Three case studies across drug user, self-help, and clinical settings document opioid withdrawal attenuation and craving reduction; richer oneirophrenic experiences correlate with longer drug-free periods.
dosing_range: 0.14–19 mg/kg oral (1962 group); 100 mg test dose + 1200 mg therapeutic (ICASH, ~1989); 100 mg + 25 mg test doses, 10 mg/kg experimental, 20 mg/kg therapeutic (Panama clinical)
aliases: Lotsof 2001; Lotsof Alexander 2001 Case Studies; Ibogaine Case Studies Patient Management

title: Ibogaine in the Treatment of Heroin Withdrawal
authors: Mash, Deborah C.; Kovera, Craig A.; Pablo, John; Tyndale, Rachel; Ervin, Frank R.; Kamlet, Jeffrey D.; Hearn, W. Lee
key_findings: PHARMACOKINETICS: Ibogaine peaks ~1h (3.2±1.3 µg/mL at 8-12mg/kg), T½ 7.3±3.2h. Noribogaine appears within 2h, persists 24-48+ hours. CYP2D6 polymorphisms affect metabolism. Establishes dose-PK relationships.

title: Ibogaine: A Novel Anti-Addictive Compound - A Comprehensive Literature Review
authors: Freedlander, Jonathan
key_findings: ACADEMIC REVIEW: Comprehensive UMBC thesis covering Lotsof history 1962-2002, pharmacology (CYP2D6→noribogaine, multi-receptor profile), 3 fatalities (1989-2000), Purkinje toxicity >75mg/kg, 18-MC as non-toxic alternative.

title: Ibogaine Related Fatality
authors: Marker, E.K.; Stajic, M.
key_findings: Ibogaine fatality in 36yo male polydrug user; tissue concentrations: subclavian blood 9225 ng/mL, brain 18563 ng/kg, liver 18137 ng/kg — extremely high levels
contraindications: Unsupervised ibogaine use; Concurrent substance abuse (morphine, cocaine metabolites detected); Polydrug use with alcohol (empty bottles at scene); Absence of medical monitoring; Chronic substance abuse history
dosing_range: unknown (ibogaine source and dose not specified; blood level 9225 ng/mL suggests massive exposure, brain/liver concentrations 18137–18563 ng/kg)
aliases: Marker 2002; Marker Stajic 2002 Ibogaine Fatality; TIAFT 2002 Ibogaine Death

title: Enantioselective Synthesis of Key Intermediates in a Novel Approach towards the Iboga-Alkaloid Family
authors: Höck, Stefan; Borschberg, Hans-Jürg
key_findings: Improved enantioselective synthesis of isoquinuclidine intermediates for iboga alkaloids from L-glutamic acid via intramolecular 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition; both antipodal series accessible.

title: Life after Ibogaine: An exploratory study of the long-term effects of ibogaine treatment on drug addicts
authors: Bastiaans, Ehud
key_findings: VU AMSTERDAM STUDY: n=21, 87% opioid primary. Drug-free periods: 1 treatment=9.7mo, 2 treatments=35.7mo, 3 treatments=52mo. HCl vs extract: 26.8 vs 2.1mo abstinence. 92% reported anxiety improvement.

title: Combating Substance Abuse with Ibogaine: Pre- and Posttreatment Recommendations and an Example of Successive Model Fitting Analyses
authors: Hittner, James B.; Quello, Susan B.
key_findings: Dose-to-weight ratio predicts heroin abstinence (sigmoidal R²=.88); threshold ~16 mg/kg; pre/posttreatment psychosocial recommendations proposed
contraindications: Active psychosis or psychosis-prone individuals (schizophrenia, bipolar disorder)
dosing_range: 700–1800 mg oral ibogaine HCl (11.7–25.0 mg/kg DWR); test dose 100–200 mg; threshold ~16 mg/kg
aliases: Hittner 2004; Ibogaine Pre-Post Treatment Recommendations; Successive Model Fitting Ibogaine

title: Glial Cell Line-Derived Neurotrophic Factor Mediates the Desirable Actions of the Anti-addiction Drug Ibogaine against Alcohol Consumption
authors: He, Dao-Yao; McGough, Nancy N. H.; Ravindranathan, Ajay; Jeanblanc, Jerome; Logrip, Marian L.; Phamluong, Khanhky; Janak, Patricia H.; Ron, Dorit
key_findings: Ibogaine reduces ethanol self-admin via VTA GDNF upregulation. Intra-VTA GDNF mimics effect; anti-GDNF antibodies attenuate it (79% vs 39% reduction). No neurotoxicity at 40 mg/kg.

title: Ibogaine in the 21st Century: Boosters, Tune-ups and Maintenance
authors: Kroupa, Patrick K.; Wells, Hattie
key_findings: Landmark MAPS Bulletin introducing booster/maintenance protocols: 500-800mg follow-up doses during noribogaine window extend benefits, reduce relapse. Clean maintenance (drug-free) vs dirty maintenance (harm reduction) protocols.
contraindications: Concurrent opiate/opioid use at higher ibogaine doses — ibogaine potentiates analgesic effect, difference between 50 mg and 500 mg described as 'quite possibly fatal'; Maintenance regimen beyond 20-25 days without break — jitteriness, sleep disturbance, overstimulation
dosing_range: Flood 16–18 mg/kg; boosters/tune-ups 500–800 mg; clean maintenance 50 mg 2x/week + 100 mg every 45 days; dirty maintenance 25–50 mg/day oral

title: Ibogaine Therapy: A 'Vast, Uncontrolled Experiment'
authors: Vastag, Brian
tags: topic/policy; topic/history; topic/opioid; topic/gdnf; topic/noribogaine; method/journalism; topic/18-mc
key_findings: News feature documenting >5000 ibogaine treatments worldwide by 2005, 30-40 clinics operating, upcoming Mash phase I trial and Israeli efficacy study, Ron GDNF findings, and Glick 18-MC development.
contraindications: Irregular cardiac rhythms (exclusion criterion at St. Kitts clinic)

title: Autoregulation of glial cell line-derived neurotrophic factor expression: implications for the long-lasting actions of the anti-addiction drug, Ibogaine
authors: He, Dao-Yao; Ron, Dorit
key_findings: FOUNDATIONAL: First demonstration that ibogaine upregulates GDNF via autoregulatory feedback loop. 10µM ibogaine induces sustained GDNF mRNA increase (48h+). GDNF positively regulates own expression - mechanism for long-term anti-addiction effects.
dosing_range: 10 µM ibogaine HCl in cell culture
aliases: He 2006; He & Ron 2006; Ibogaine GDNF Autoregulation

title: Simultaneous quantification of ibogaine and noribogaine in human plasma and whole blood by liquid chromatography with electrospray ionization mass spectrometric detection
authors: Kontrimavičiūtė, Vilma; Breton, Hervé; Mathieu, Olivier; Mathieu-Daudé, Jean Claude; Bressolle, Françoise M.M.
key_findings: First validated LC-ESI-MS method for ibogaine/noribogaine in plasma (LLOQ 0.89/1 µg/L) and whole blood (LLOQ 1.78/2 µg/kg). Fatal poisoning case: blood levels 10.8 µg/g ibogaine, 20.8 µg/g noribogaine (10–20× therapeutic).

title: Distribution of Ibogaine and Noribogaine in a Man Following a Poisoning Involving Root Bark of the Tabernanthe iboga Shrub
authors: Kontrimavičiūtė, Violeta; Mathieu, Olivier; Mathieu-Daudé, Jean-Claude; Vainauskas, Paulius; Casper, Thierry; Baccino, Eric; Bressolle, Françoise M.M.
key_findings: First human tissue distribution of ibogaine/noribogaine post-mortem. Highest concentrations in lung (50.1/55.9 µg/g), liver (40.5/50.5 µg/g), brain (12.5/18.7 µg/g). Blood levels 5–20× therapeutic range.
contraindications: Unsupervised root bark ingestion; Concomitant drug abuse history; Absence of medical monitoring
dosing_range: unknown (root bark, ~18 spoonfuls over ~10 hours, blood levels 5–20× therapeutic)

title: Fatalities after taking ibogaine in addiction treatment could be related to sudden cardiac death caused by autonomic dysfunction
authors: Maas, Uwe; Strubelt, Sünje
key_findings: Hypothesises ibogaine fatalities are sudden cardiac deaths from autonomic dysregulation; Bwiti ritual maintains right-hemispheric trance as cardioprotective mechanism
contraindications: Pre-existing cardiovascular disease; Epilepsy (associated autonomic dysfunction); Psychiatric conditions with autonomic dysregulation (schizophrenia, PTSD, depression)
dosing_range: variable — fatalities after doses from sub-therapeutic (<10 mg/kg) to standard therapeutic (20–30 mg/kg); exact doses often unknown in retrospective cases
aliases: Maas 2006; Maas & Strubelt 2006; Autonomic Dysfunction Hypothesis

title: Ibogaine affects brain energy metabolism
authors: Paškulin, Roman; Jamnik, Polona; Živin, Marko; Raspor, Peter; Štrukelj, Borut
key_findings: NOVEL MECHANISM: Ibogaine induces glycolysis/TCA cycle enzymes (GAPDH, aldolase A, pyruvate kinase, malate dehydrogenase) at 24-72h. Anti-addiction effect may be mediated by enhanced energy availability for neuroplasticity.

title: The Use of Ibogaine in the Treatment of Addictions
authors: Alper, Kenneth R.; Lotsof, Howard S.
key_findings: DEFINITIVE CLINICAL OVERVIEW: Comprehensive Alper/Lotsof chapter covering mechanisms, dosing protocols, safety considerations, global treatment landscape, and NIDA research history. Key reference for ibogaine clinical practice.

title: The ibogaine medical subculture
authors: Alper, Kenneth R.; Lotsof, Howard S.; Kaplan, Charles D.
key_findings: 3414 individuals took ibogaine by Feb 2006 (4× increase over 5yr); 68% for substance disorders, 53% for opioid withdrawal; 11 fatalities reported 1990–2006, mostly cardiac-related with pre-existing risk factors
contraindications: Cardiac disease (prior MI, cardiomyopathy, valvular disease); Acute hepatitis; Psychotic disorders
aliases: Alper 2008; Ibogaine Medical Subculture; Alper Lotsof Kaplan 2008

title: Determination of ibogaine and noribogaine in biological fluids and hair by LC–MS/MS after Tabernanthe iboga abuse: Iboga alkaloids distribution in a drowning death case
authors: Chèze, Marjorie; Lenoan, Aurélie; Deveaux, Marc; Pépin, Gilbert
key_findings: Post-mortem ibogaine/noribogaine distribution in 9 tissue types after iboga-related drowning; first report of ibogaine detection in hair (1.2/2.5 ng/mg)
contraindications: Pre-existing myocardial bridging; Unsupervised iboga root bark ingestion; Absence of medical monitoring; Use in uncontrolled settings (Bwiti ceremony context)
dosing_range: unknown (iboga root bark; blood levels elevated relative to published therapeutic range — femoral ibogaine 3.3 µg/mL, noribogaine 4.6 µg/mL)
aliases: Chèze 2008; Ibogaine Drowning Forensic Toxicology; Ibogaine Hair Analysis Case

title: Ibogaine, an anti-addictive drug: pharmacology and time to go further in development. A narrative review
authors: Mačiulaitis, R; Kontrimavičiūtė, V; Bressolle, FMM; Briedis, V
key_findings: Comprehensive pharmacology review: noribogaine 10× more potent at SERT (IC₅₀ 0.04 vs 0.59 μM), full μ-opioid agonist (IC₅₀ 0.16 vs 11.0 μM); CYP2D6 polymorphism yields 9× lower noribogaine Cmax in PMs
contraindications: CYP2D6 poor metaboliser status (altered ibogaine/noribogaine ratio); Pre-existing cardiac conditions (dose-dependent cardiotoxicity in preclinical models); Concomitant hepatically-metabolised drugs (CYP2D6 pathway)

title: Long-QT Syndrome Induced by the Antiaddiction Drug Ibogaine
authors: Hoelen, Dianne W.M.; Spiering, Wilko; Valk, Gerlof D.
key_findings: First published case of ibogaine-induced Long-QT syndrome (QTc 616 ms) with ventricular tachyarrhythmias in a 31-year-old woman; QT normalised at 42 hours; concurrent hypomagnesaemia and hypokalaemia identified.
contraindications: Pre-existing QT prolongation; Electrolyte imbalance (hypomagnesaemia, hypokalaemia)

title: Iboga: Between Myth and Reality
authors: Paškulin, Roman
key_findings: COMPREHENSIVE REVIEW: French-language synthesis of iboga's dual identity - traditional Bwiti medicine (convalescence, fatigue, spiritual ritual) and modern anti-addiction research. Bridges cultural and scientific perspectives.

title: Interaction of ibogaine with human α3β4-nicotinic acetylcholine receptors in different conformational states
authors: Arias, Hugo R.; Rosenberg, Avraham; Targowska-Duda, Katarzyna M.; Feuerbach, Dominik; Yuan, Xiao Juan; Jozwiak, Krzysztof; Moaddel, Ruin; Wainer, Irving W.
key_findings: RECEPTOR MECHANISM: Ibogaine inhibits human α3β4 nAChRs with state-dependent binding - higher affinity for desensitised states. Noncompetitive mechanism supports nicotinic modulation as key anti-addiction pathway.
journal: International Journal of Biochemistry & Cell Biology

title: Noribogaine, but not 18-MC, exhibits similar actions as ibogaine on GDNF expression and ethanol self-administration
authors: Carnicella, Sebastien; He, Dao-Yao; Yowell, Quinn V.; Glick, Stanley D.; Ron, Dorit
tags: topic/noribogaine; topic/18-mc; topic/gdnf; topic/alcohol; mechanism/dopamine-modulation; method/preclinical
key_findings: Noribogaine increases GDNF expression like ibogaine; 18-MC does not. Intra-VTA noribogaine reduces alcohol self-administration; 18-MC does not. Different mechanisms despite similar anti-addictive profiles. Key for analogue development.

title: Induction of energy metabolism related enzymes in yeast Saccharomyces cerevisiae exposed to ibogaine is adaptation to acute decrease in ATP energy pool
authors: Paškulin, Roman; Jamnik, Polona; Obermajer, Nataša; Slavić, Marija; Štrukelj, Borut
key_findings: MECHANISM CONFIRMATION: Yeast model shows ibogaine causes acute ATP decrease triggering compensatory enzyme induction. Supports energy metabolism hypothesis - neuroplasticity may require enhanced metabolic capacity.

title: Psychedelics and the Human Receptorome
authors: Ray, Thomas S.
key_findings: NIMH-PDSP receptorome study: 35 psychedelics at 51 receptor sites. Table S1 comprehensive ibogaine/noribogaine Ki data: high σ2, κ-opioid, SERT affinity. Novel Ki normalization method for comparing multi-receptor profiles.

title: Ibogaine and the inhibition of acetylcholinesterase
authors: Alper, Kenneth R.; Reith, Maarten E. A.; Sershen, Henry
key_findings: NOVEL MECHANISM: Ibogaine inhibits acetylcholinesterase, contributing to muscarinic cholinergic effects. Adds to multi-receptor profile and may explain some pharmacological/toxicological properties.

title: The Need for Ibogaine in Drug and Alcohol Addiction Treatment
authors: Donnelly, Jennifer R.
tags: topic/policy; topic/opioid; topic/harm-reduction; topic/history; topic/alcohol; method/review
key_findings: Legal commentary argues ibogaine should be rescheduled from Schedule I to II in the US, citing 83% withdrawal-free outcomes, societal costs of $181B/yr for drug abuse, and low abuse potential
contraindications: Significantly impaired liver function; ECG abnormalities or previous heart problems; Severe mental health problems (schizophrenia, bipolar disorder); HIV positive or Hepatitis C symptomatic; Current antipsychotic medication use; Long-term medication with no prior data on ibogaine interactions; Narcotic analgesics, cocaine, amphetamines, or alcohol use within 12 hours prior to treatment
aliases: Donnelly 2011; Ibogaine Drug Alcohol Addiction Legal Commentary; Need for Ibogaine in Addiction Treatment

title: Ibogaine-Associated Psychosis in Schizophrenia: A Case Report
authors: Houenou, Josselin; Homri, Wided; Leboyer, Marion; Drancourt, Noémie
key_findings: First report of ibogaine-associated psychosis in schizophrenia; recommends excluding schizophrenia from ibogaine trials and screening for ibogaine use in shamanic-ritual psychosis
contraindications: Schizophrenia or psychotic disorders; Attenuated psychotic symptoms or prodromal psychosis; Active psychosis; Unsupervised consumption outside clinical setting; Daily or chronic ibogaine use
dosing_range: unquantified (Iboga powder, small amounts escalating to daily use over 2 months)
aliases: Houenou 2011; Ibogaine Psychosis Schizophrenia Case Report

title: The Visionary Cure of the Addiction War? Ibogaine: Social Context, Subcultural Identity, and Implications for Drug Policy
authors: Rodger, James
tags: topic/policy; topic/history; topic/traditional-use; topic/harm-reduction; topic/opioid; method/qualitative
key_findings: Ibogaine treatment attracts addicts through subcultural identity formation that inverts dominant culture values; 3,414 treatments by 2006, 12 deaths since 1990
aliases: Rodger 2011; Ibogaine Social Context Subcultural Identity

title: Fatalities Temporally Associated with the Ingestion of Ibogaine
authors: Alper, Kenneth R.; Stajić, Marina; Gill, James R.
key_findings: FOUNDATIONAL: Systematic review of all 19 ibogaine fatalities (1990-2008). 12/14 adequate autopsies showed cardiovascular comorbidities + polysubstance intoxication explained deaths. No characteristic neurotoxicity syndrome.
contraindications: cardiovascular disease; coronary artery atherosclerosis; dilated cardiomyopathy; ventricular hypertrophy; liver disease; opioid co-use; benzodiazepine withdrawal; alcohol withdrawal; cocaine use; hypokalemia; hypomagnesemia

title: Ibogaine
authors: Booze, Lisa
key_findings: Maryland Poison Center alert: ibogaine toxicity includes QTc prolongation, bradycardia, VT, TdP, respiratory arrest; cardiac effects may persist up to 9 days; no specific antidote
contraindications: Pre-existing cardiac disease; Concomitant use of opioids (active use or inadequate taper); Concomitant use of benzodiazepines or cocaine; Seizure risk from alcohol or benzodiazepine withdrawal; Use of ibogaine extract or root bark instead of ibogaine HCl; Administration without close medical observation and monitoring
dosing_range: not specified (administered as ibogaine HCl 95–98% pure, ibogaine extracts, or dried root bark containing 15–50% iboga alkaloids)
aliases: Booze 2012; Maryland Poison Center Ibogaine Toxicity Alert; ToxTidbits Ibogaine 2012

title: Anti-addiction drug ibogaine inhibits hERG channels: a cardiac arrhythmia risk
authors: Koenig, Xaver; Kovar, Michael; Boehm, Stefan; Sandtner, Walter; Hilber, Karlheinz
key_findings: Ibogaine blocks hERG channels with IC₅₀ of 4 μM, overlapping therapeutic plasma concentrations (low μM range), providing first mechanistic evidence for ibogaine-induced QT prolongation and cardiac arrhythmia risk.
contraindications: Pre-existing cardiac conditions; QT-prolonging medications
dosing_range: IC₅₀ 3.9 μM — in vitro hERG patch-clamp; overlaps therapeutic plasma range (low μM)
aliases: Koenig 2012; Ibogaine hERG Channel Blockade Brief Communication

title: Life-threatening complications of ibogaine: three case reports
authors: Paling, F.P.; Andrews, L.M.; Valk, G.D.; Blom, H.J.
tags: topic/adverse-event; topic/toxicity; topic/opioid; topic/alcohol; mechanism/herg-blockade; method/case-report
key_findings: Three Dutch case reports: QTc >700ms with torsade de pointes, respiratory depression requiring ventilation. Naranjo scale confirms probable ibogaine causality. Authors warn of unregulated online availability.
contraindications: hypokalaemia; hypomagnesaemia; opioid co-use; alcohol dependence; benzodiazepine dependence

title: Metabolic plasticity and the energy economizing effect of ibogaine, the principal alkaloid of Tabernanthe iboga
authors: Paškulin, Roman; Jamnik, Polona; Danevčič, Tjaša; Koželj, Gordana; Krašovec, Rok; Krstić-Milošević, Dijana; Blagojević, Duško; Štrukelj, Borut
key_findings: Ibogaine triggers dose-dependent biphasic CO₂ elevation in yeast (peak +142% at 20 mg/l), reduces oxidative load 24–57% via pro-antioxidant mechanism, and establishes lower-energy metabolic equilibrium.

title: Ibogaine-associated ventricular tachyarrhythmias
authors: Pleskovic, Ales; Gorjup, Vojka; Brvar, Miran; Kozelj, Gordana
key_findings: Single 600mg ibogaine dose caused 5 VFs and 3 VTs over 48h with QTc prolongation to 593ms at 42h. Amiodarone worsened QTc. Vagal maneuvers triggered arrhythmias. QTc normalised day 9.
contraindications: QTc-prolonging medications (e.g., amiodarone, methadone); Pre-existing QT prolongation; Recent polysubstance use

title: Pause-dependent ventricular tachycardia and torsades de pointes after ibogaine ingestion
authors: Shawn, Lauren K.; Alper, Kenneth; Desai, Shalin P.; Stephenson, Kent; Olgun, Aydin M.; Nelson, Lewis S.; Hoffman, Robert S.
key_findings: First documented case of pause-dependent VT and TdP after ibogaine in a patient with normal pretreatment screening, demonstrating screening limitations.
contraindications: Concurrent or recent methadone use (patient transitioned off one month prior); Aggressive cathartic use prior to ibogaine (caused persistent hypokalemia); Combined ibogaine HCl and root bark extract administration (dual-source dosing); Non-medical setting without continuous cardiac monitoring; History of IV heroin use
dosing_range: 10.5 mg/kg ibogaine HCl (85–98% purity) + 14 mg/kg T. iboga root bark extract (50% alkaloids) over 15 hours
aliases: Shawn 2012; Ibogaine TdP VT Normal Screening; NACCT 2012 Abstract 180

title: The Vanishing World of Iboga: And a Closer Look at Sustainable Ibogaine
authors: Sisko, Bob
key_findings: Conference presentation on T. iboga endangerment from over-harvesting. Documents black market crisis in Gabon/Cameroon. Discusses Voacanga africana as sustainable alternative source for ibogaine synthesis.

title: Ibogaine for opioid addiction: A Deadly treatment
authors: Warrick, Brandon; Baltarowich, Lydia
key_findings: Fatal ibogaine case in 25-year-old with pre-existing cardiac dysrhythmias and AICD; postmortem ibogaine levels: heart blood 2.2 mcg/ml, liver 4.2 mcg/g.
contraindications: Pre-existing cardiac dysrhythmias with AICD placement; Unsupervised self-treatment without medical monitoring; History of heroin abuse (substance use history); No pretreatment cardiac screening performed; Internet-sourced ibogaine of unverified composition
aliases: Warrick 2012; Ibogaine Fatality AICD; NACCT 2012 Abstract 184

title: Effect of Iboga Alkaloids on µ-Opioid Receptor-Coupled G Protein Activation
authors: Antonio, Tamara; Childers, Steven R.; Rothman, Richard B.; Dersch, Christina M.; King, Christine; Kuehne, Martin; Bornmann, William G.; Eshleman, Amy J.; Janowsky, Aaron; Simon, Eric R.; Reith, Maarten E. A.; Alper, Kenneth
key_findings: CRITICAL MECHANISM FINDING: Ibogaine, noribogaine, 18-MC are MOR ANTAGONISTS (Ki 3-13µM), NOT agonists. Opioid withdrawal attenuation cannot be explained by MOR agonism - novel mechanism required.

title: Growing Menace of Ibogaine Toxicity
authors: Asua, Ignacio
key_findings: First UK ibogaine toxicity case: 7g oral dose caused QTc 600ms, TdP cardiac arrest, seizures; rescued by defibrillation and transcutaneous overdrive pacing at 80 bpm for 48h.
contraindications: Pre-existing cardiac conditions; Concurrent opioid or methadone use; Unsupervised self-administration
aliases: Asua 2013; UK Ibogaine Toxicity Case; Ibogaine TdP Cardiac Arrest

title: Ibogaine in the Treatment of Substance Dependence
authors: Brown, Thomas Kingsley
tags: topic/opioid; topic/harm-reduction; topic/18-mc; topic/noribogaine; topic/adverse-event; topic/cardiac; topic/alcohol; topic/phenomenology; topic/history; method/review
key_findings: Comprehensive narrative review covering ibogaine history, preclinical evidence, safety concerns (19 fatalities to 2008), and human efficacy data; identifies ibogaine medical subculture growth and need for controlled trials.
aliases: Brown 2013; T.K. Brown 2013 CDAR review; Ibogaine treatment substance dependence review

title: Unique and potent effects of acute ibogaine on zebrafish: The developing utility of novel aquatic models for hallucinogenic drug research
authors: Cachat, Jonathan; Kyzar, Evan J.; Collins, Christopher; Gaikwad, Siddharth; Green, Jeremy; Roth, Andrew; El-Ounsi, Mohamed; Davis, Ari; Pham, Mimi; Landsman, Samuel; Stewart, Adam Michael; Kalueff, Allan V.
key_findings: Zebrafish ibogaine exposure (10–20 mg/L) reverses geotaxis and scototaxis, disrupts shoaling, evokes melanophore darkening, and validates aquatic 3D phenotyping for hallucinogen research.
aliases: Cachat 2013; Cachat Zebrafish 2013; Ibogaine Zebrafish Behavioural Pharmacology

title: A Case of Death Due to Ibogaine Use for Heroin Addiction
authors: Jalal, Shwan; Daher, Edouard; Hilu, Raymond
key_findings: 25yo male with pre-existing SVT died from multi-organ failure 2 days after ingesting 2.5g ibogaine for heroin cessation; unsupervised setting; highlights pre-existing cardiac conditions as critical risk factor
contraindications: Pre-existing supraventricular tachycardia (SVT); Pre-existing cardiac arrhythmias

title: Anti-addiction drug ibogaine inhibits voltage-gated ionic currents: A study to assess the drug's cardiac ion channel profile
authors: Koenig, Xaver; Kovar, Michael; Rubi, Lena; Mike, Agnes K.; Lukacs, Peter; Gawali, Vaibhavkumar S.; Todt, Hannes; Hilber, Karlheinz; Sandtner, Walter
key_findings: Ibogaine IC₅₀: hERG 4 μM, hNaᵥ1.5 142 μM, hCaᵥ1.2 163 μM. Free plasma 1–11 μM at therapeutic dose. Computer model predicts human AP prolongation despite Caᵥ1.2 counteraction. 18-MC hERG IC₅₀ 15 μM.
contraindications: Pre-existing cardiac conditions; Concurrent use of QT-prolonging drugs; Hypokalaemia (exacerbates hERG blockade and reduces channel surface density)
dosing_range: IC₅₀: hERG 4 μM, hNav1.5 142 μM, hCav1.2 163 μM; 18-MC hERG 15 μM — in vitro concentrations
aliases: Koenig 2013; Ibogaine Cardiac Ion Channel Profile

title: Fatal Case of a 27-Year-Old Male After Taking Iboga in Withdrawal Treatment: GC-MS/MS Determination of Ibogaine and Ibogamine in Iboga Roots and Postmortem Biological Material
authors: Mazoyer, Cédric; Carlier, Jérémy; Boucher, Alexandra; Péoc'h, Michel; Lemeur, Catherine; Gaillard, Yvan
key_findings: FATALITY CASE: 27yo male died ~12h after iboga root ingestion during withdrawal treatment. Femoral blood ibogaine 1.27 μg/mL. Co-ingestion with methadone and diazepam — triple QT-prolongation risk.
contraindications: Concurrent methadone use (dual QT-prolongation risk); Concurrent benzodiazepine use (potentiates methadone cardiotoxicity); Unsupervised treatment setting without medical monitoring; Unknown or unverified dose of crude iboga root bark; Polysubstance addiction history (15 years)
dosing_range: Unknown; claimed 'teaspoon' (~108–144 mg ibogaine from 1.5–2g powder at 7.2%), but blood levels suggest substantially higher dose (possibly >20g powder)
aliases: Mazoyer 2013; Mazoyer Fatality 2013; French Ibogaine Fatality 2006

title: Ibogaine related sudden death: A case report
authors: Papadodima, Stavroula A.; Dona, Artemis; Evaggelakos, Christos I.; Goutas, Nikolaos; Athanaselis, Sotirios A.
key_findings: 52yo alcoholic male died 12–24h post-ibogaine (blood 2 mg/L). Autopsy: liver cirrhosis (>90% fatty infiltration), 40–45% coronary occlusion, myocardial ischaemia. Death classified cardiac, ibogaine-related.
contraindications: Pre-existing cardiovascular disease; Liver cirrhosis / hepatic disease; Chronic alcoholism; Hypokalemia; Hypomagnesemia
journal: Journal of Forensic and Legal Medicine
aliases: Papadodima 2013; Ibogaine sudden death Greece case

title: Ibogaine Treatment for Substance Dependence: Legality, Social Geography, and Efficacy
authors: Kingsley Brown, Thomas
key_findings: COMPREHENSIVE REVIEW: Global ibogaine medical subculture development, legal geography (Mexico/Canada/NZ vs US Schedule I), sustainability concerns, Baja California long-term outcomes research.
journal: II Congreso Internacional de medicina tradicional y salud pública

title: Ibogaine Effect on Cocaine Craving and Use in Dependent Patients - A Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Pilot Study
authors: Prior, Pedro Luis; Prior, Sergio Luiz
key_findings: Single 1800mg ibogaine dose significantly reduced cocaine craving (MCCS 7.4→1.3 at 72h, p<0.0001) and positive urine samples vs placebo over 24 weeks in N=20 RCT.
contraindications: Current alcohol or other drug dependence (other than nicotine); Significant cardiovascular conditions or current cardiac condition; Renal disorders; Endocrine disorders; Hepatic disorders (SGOT or SGPT >3× normal); Hypotension or hypertension; Seizure disorder; Lifetime diagnosis of schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, or other psychotic disorders; Active suicidality or homicidality; Current prescription for psychotropic medication
dosing_range: 1800 mg single dose (dried ibogaine extract, 75% purity, encapsulated with excipients)
journal: Jacobs Journal of Addiction and Therapy

title: Treating drug dependence with the aid of ibogaine: A retrospective study
authors: Schenberg, Eduardo Ekman; de Castro Comis, Maria Angélica; Rasmussen Chaves, Bruno; da Silveira, Dartiu Xavier
key_findings: Brazilian retrospective (n=75, 134 sessions): 61% abstinent, median 5.5mo (single) vs 8.4mo (multiple tx, p<0.001). Zero serious AEs or fatalities. First systematic non-opioid dependence data.
contraindications: Pregnancy; Surgery within 6 months; Uncontrolled hypertension; Uncontrolled diabetes; Cardiac arrhythmias; Renal insufficiency; Hepatic insufficiency; Alzheimer's disease; Parkinson's disease; Psychiatric comorbidities; Active substance use (30–60 day abstinence period required prior to administration)
aliases: Schenberg 2014; Brazilian Retrospective Ibogaine Study; Schenberg Retrospective 2014

title: Mechanism of hERG Channel Block by the Psychoactive Indole Alkaloid Ibogaine
authors: Thurner, Patrick; Stary-Weinzinger, Anna; Gafar, Hend; Gawali, Vaibhavkumar S.; Kudlacek, Oliver; Zezula, Juergen; Hilber, Karlheinz; Boehm, Stefan; Sandtner, Walter; Koenig, Xaver
key_findings: Ibogaine blocks hERG channels from cytosolic side via open/inactivated-state binding at Y652/F656 residues; IC₅₀ ~1-4 μM; F656A mutation reduces potency 50-fold; inactivation-deficient mutant equally sensitive.
contraindications: Pre-existing hERG channel dysfunction; Concomitant use of other hERG-blocking drugs; Conditions favouring intracellular acidosis (increases ibogaine trapping and hERG block)
dosing_range: IC₅₀ ~1–4 μM (hERG wild-type); standard test concentration 3 μM — in vitro
journal: Journal of Pharmacology and Experimental Therapeutics
aliases: Thurner 2014; hERG channel block mechanism ibogaine

title: Cardiac Arrest After Ibogaine Ingestion
authors: Vlaanderen, L.; Martial, L. C.; Franssen, E. J. F.; van der Voort, P. H. J.; Oosterwerff, E.; Somsen, G. A.
key_findings: Cardiac arrest from VF in healthy 26M ~5h post 2400 mg ibogaine (32.4 mg/kg); QTc max 663 ms at 18h; serum ibogaine 948 µg/L; permanent neurological deficits from post-anoxic encephalopathy

title: Herbal seizures - atypical symptoms after ibogaine intoxication: a case report
authors: Breuer, Lorenz; Kasper, Burkhard S.; Schwarze, Bernd; Gschossmann, Juergen M.; Kornhuber, Johannes; Müller, Helge H.
key_findings: SEIZURE CASE: 22yo male, extreme 38g dose triggered generalised tonic-clonic seizures. Suggests ibogaine proconvulsive at high doses. Treated with midazolam/levetiracetam.
contraindications: Seizure disorders or epilepsy; High-dose iboga root bark ingestion (proconvulsive at high doses)
dosing_range: 38g root bark (~35mg/kg estimated at 7% ibogaine content)

title: Deconstructing the Iboga Alkaloid Skeleton: Potentiation of FGF2-induced Glial Cell Line-Derived Neurotrophic Factor Release by a Novel Compound
authors: Gassaway, Madalee M.; Jacques, Teresa L.; Kruegel, Andrew C.; Karpowicz, Richard J. Jr.; Li, Xiaoguang; Li, Shu; Myer, Yves; Sames, Dalibor
key_findings: Novel acyclic iboga analogue XL-008 potentiates FGF2-induced GDNF release >2-fold potency and >3-fold efficacy in C6 glioma cells via MEK/PI3K pathways. Sames lab, Columbia.

title: Influence of CYP2D6 Activity on the Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics of a Single 20 mg Dose of Ibogaine in Healthy Volunteers
authors: Glue, Paul; Winter, Helen; Garbe, Kira; Jakobi, Hannah; Lyudin, Alexander; Lenagh-Glue, Zoe; Hung, C. Tak
key_findings: CYP2D6 CRITICAL: Paroxetine pretreatment (CYP2D6 inhibition) doubled active moiety exposure. Recommends genotyping + halved doses for CYP2D6 poor metabolisers. 20mg safe in healthy volunteers.
aliases: Glue 2015 CYP2D6; Glue CYP2D6 2015; Ibogaine CYP2D6 Study; Glue2015_CYP2D6_Ibogaine_PK

title: Ascending-Dose Study of Noribogaine in Healthy Volunteers: Pharmacokinetics, Pharmacodynamics, Safety, and Tolerability
authors: Glue, Paul; Lockhart, Michelle; Lam, Fred; Hung, Noelyn; Hung, Cheung-Tak; Friedhoff, Lawrence
key_findings: First-in-human noribogaine Phase I: 3-60mg doses safe, well-tolerated. T½=28-49h, high Vd=1417-3086L, dose-linear PK. No mu-opioid agonist effects. Establishes human safety profile.
aliases: Glue 2015; Glue Noribogaine 2015; Noribogaine Phase I; Glue2015_Ascending_Dose_Noribogaine_Safety

title: A Case of QT Prolongation and Torsades de Pointes Caused by Ibogaine Toxicity
authors: Hildyard, Catherine; Macklin, Philip; Prendergast, Bernard; Bashir, Yaver
key_findings: 39yo male: QTc 640→730ms (day 2)→400ms (day 7) after 7g oral ibogaine. 8 episodes polymorphic VT/TdP, normal electrolytes. Suppressed by IV Mg 2g + transvenous overdrive pacing at 80bpm.
contraindications: Pre-existing QT prolongation; Concurrent use of QT-prolonging drugs; History of seizures
aliases: Hildyard 2015; Ibogaine QT Prolongation TdP Case Oxford

title: The Anti-Addiction Drug Ibogaine and the Heart: A Delicate Relation
authors: Koenig, Xaver; Hilber, Karlheinz
tags: topic/cardiac; topic/adverse-event; topic/toxicity; topic/electrolytes; topic/noribogaine; topic/pharmacokinetics; mechanism/herg-blockade; mechanism/ion-channel; method/review
key_findings: Comprehensive cardiac review: hERG IC₅₀ ~4 μM overlaps therapeutic range; 22 fatalities tabulated with 100% hypokalemia when tested; noribogaine half-life (28–49 h) extends cardiac risk beyond acute phase.
contraindications: hypokalemia; hypomagnesemia; cardiovascular disease; prolonged baseline QT interval; bradycardia; female gender; hERG channel mutations; CYP2D6 inhibitors; methadone co-use; cocaine co-use; diazepam co-use; hepatic impairment (hepatitis, liver cirrhosis); concurrent QT-prolonging medications; unsupervised or unmonitored administration
aliases: Koenig-Hilber 2015; Koenig-Hilber; König-Hilber 2015; Ibogaine and the Heart Review; Koenig2015_Anti_Addiction_Drug_Ibogaine_Heart; Koenig2015_Ibogaine_Heart_Mechanisms

title: Mania following use of ibogaine: A case series
authors: Marta, Cole J.; Ryan, Wesley C.; Kopelowicz, Alex; Koek, Ralph J.
key_findings: SAFETY ALERT: 3 cases de novo mania post-ibogaine in patients with no prior bipolar history. Naranjo scale: probable (2 cases) and possible (1). First published reports of ibogaine-induced mania.
contraindications: Personal or family history of bipolar disorder or mania; Prior manic or psychotic episodes; Concurrent or recent psilocybin use (confounding psychiatric risk); Unregulated treatment setting without psychiatric monitoring or follow-up
dosing_range: Unknown in all three cases (unregulated settings; doses not reported or verified)
aliases: Marta 2015; Ibogaine Mania Case Series; Ibogaine-Induced Mania

title: Ex vivo effects of ibogaine on the activity of antioxidative enzymes in human erythrocytes
authors: Nikolić-Kokić, Aleksandra; Oreščanin-Dušić, Zorana; Spasojević, Ivan; Slavić, Marija; Mijušković, Ana; Paškulin, Roman; Miljević, Čedo; Spasić, Mihajlo B.; Blagojević, Duško P.
key_findings: Ex vivo ibogaine (10-20μM) elevates SOD1 and GR in human erythrocytes, increases plasma ATP dose-dependently, stabilises SOD1 against H₂O₂ — pro-antioxidant via energy metabolism.
dosing_range: 10–20 µM ibogaine HCl (human erythrocytes ex vivo)

title: Internet-purchased ibogaine toxicity confirmed with serum, urine, and product content levels
authors: O'Connell, Charles W.; Gerona, Roy R.; Friesen, Matthew W.; Ly, Binh T.
key_findings: Acute ibogaine toxicity: peak serum 377 ng/mL, QTc 527 ms (transient), non-linear elimination (t½ 67–162 min), estimated 3.8 g ingested from internet-purchased capsules (479 mg/capsule).
contraindications: Pre-existing cardiac conditions; Uncontrolled/unsupervised dosing; Concurrent opioid use
dosing_range: ~3.8 g oral (8 capsules × 479 mg, over <6 hours)

title: hERG Blockade by Iboga Alkaloids
authors: Alper, Kenneth; Bai, Rong; Liu, Nian; Fowler, Steven J.; Huang, Xi-Ping; Priori, Silvia G.; Ruan, Yanfei
key_findings: LANDMARK hERG DATA: Whole-cell patch clamp IC₅₀ values - ibogaine 4.09μM, noribogaine 2.86μM, voacangine 2.25μM. 18-MC >50μM (12× safer). Noribogaine's extended T½ may explain delayed cardiac events.
contraindications: hypokalemia; hypomagnesemia; cardiac disease; bradycardia; liver disease; respiratory disease; seizures; alcohol withdrawal; cocaine withdrawal; co-ingestants
dosing_range: 10–25 mg/kg (typical clinical range); IC₅₀ overlaps therapeutic plasma concentrations (2.4 μM ibogaine, 3.2 μM noribogaine at 10 mg/kg)
aliases: Alper 2016; Alper; Alper hERG 2016; Alper2016_hERG_Blockade_Iboga_Alkaloids; Alper2016_hERG_Ibogaine

title: Ibogaine and addiction in the animal model, a systematic review and meta-analysis
authors: Belgers, M.; Leenaars, M.; Homberg, J. R.; Ritskes-Hoitinga, M.; Schellekens, A. F. A.; Hooijmans, C. R.
key_findings: SYRCLE META-ANALYSIS: 27 studies show ibogaine reduces drug self-administration especially in first 24h. No effect on CPP. Motor impairment and cerebellar toxicity noted. Warrants clinical trials with monitoring.

title: Remission of Severe Opioid Use Disorder with Ibogaine: A Case Report
authors: Cloutier-Gill, Laurie; Wood, Evan; Millar, Trevor; Ferris, Caroline; Socias, M. Eugenia
key_findings: Treatment-refractory OUD patient (19yr history) achieved 18-month abstinence after 4-day ibogaine HCl treatment (32 mg/kg total). Mild bradycardia observed (51–67 bpm). Patient attributed success to transformative experience over withdrawal relief.
contraindications: Pre-existing cardiovascular disease; Concurrent QTc-prolonging substances; Electrolyte imbalances (hypokalemia)
aliases: Cloutier-Gill 2016; Ibogaine OUD Case Report Vancouver

title: Effects of low dose ibogaine on subjective mood state and psychological performance
authors: Forsyth, Bridget; Machado, Liana; Jowett, Tim; Jakobi, Hannah; Garbe, Kira; Winter, Helen; Glue, Paul
key_findings: Single 20 mg ibogaine had no effect on mood or most cognitive functions in 21 healthy males; only Simon Congruent RT showed concentration-response association with active moiety (adjusted p=0.016).
aliases: Forsyth 2016; Forsyth Low Dose Ibogaine Cognition; Otago Ibogaine Psychological Performance

title: Ascending Single-Dose, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Safety Study of Noribogaine in Opioid-Dependent Patients
authors: Glue, Paul; Cape, Gavin; Tunnicliff, Donna; Lockhart, Michelle; Lam, Fred; Hung, Noelyn; Hung, C. Tak; Harland, Sarah; Devane, Jane; Crockett, R. S.; Howes, John; Darpo, Borje; Zhou, Meijian; Weis, Holger; Friedhoff, Lawrence
key_findings: First-in-patient RCT of noribogaine (60-180mg) in 27 opioid-dependent patients. Concentration-dependent QTcI prolongation (0.17 ms/ng/mL); mean ΔΔQTcI 16/28/42 ms at 60/120/180mg. t½ 24-30h, dose-linear PK.
contraindications: Pre-existing QTc prolongation; Concurrent QT-prolonging medications; Risk factors for torsades de pointes
aliases: Glue 2016; Noribogaine first-in-patient safety; DemeRx noribogaine trial

title: Switching Opioid-Dependent Patients From Methadone to Morphine: Safety, Tolerability, and Methadone Pharmacokinetics
authors: Glue, Paul; Cape, Gavin; Tunnicliff, Donna; Lockhart, Michelle; Lam, Fred; Gray, Andrew; Hung, Noelyn; Hung, C. Tak; Harland, Sarah; Devane, Jane; Howes, John; Weis, Holger; Friedhoff, Lawrence
key_findings: 7-day methadone-to-morphine switch protocol: 27/27 completed with no dropouts. SR morphine at 5.2× methadone dose adequate. Methadone t½ 59h; 91% cleared in 7 days. No safety issues; no QTcF >500ms.
dosing_range: SR morphine 4–5× methadone dose (mean 311 mg/day); clonidine 200 μg/day patch PRN
aliases: Glue 2016b; Methadone to morphine switch protocol; DemeRx methadone switch

title: How toxic is ibogaine?
authors: Litjens, Ruud P.W.; Brunt, Tibor M.
tags: topic/toxicity; topic/cardiac; topic/pharmacokinetics; topic/noribogaine; topic/adverse-event; mechanism/herg-blockade; method/review
key_findings: Reviews 27 ibogaine fatalities and 8 cardiac case reports (QTc 460–730ms) in patients WITHOUT pre-existing conditions. CYP2D6 polymorphism affects toxicity. Noribogaine at least as cardiotoxic as ibogaine.
contraindications: Pre-existing cardiovascular conditions (cardiomyopathy, prior infarction, coronary stenosis); QT interval prolongation or family history of cardiac-rhythm abnormalities; CYP2D6 poor metaboliser status (requires ≥50% dose reduction); Concurrent medications metabolised by CYP2D6 (e.g., paroxetine doubles active moiety); Concurrent methadone or benzodiazepine use; Concurrent buprenorphine use (CYP3A4 competition slows ibogaine clearance); Concurrent use of other QT-prolonging substances; Unsupervised or limited medical supervision settings
dosing_range: 10–25 mg/kg therapeutic; toxicity cases typically >2g; Lambarène 8mg/tablet historical
aliases: Litjens 2016; How Toxic Is Ibogaine Review

title: Oral noribogaine shows high brain uptake and anti-withdrawal effects not associated with place preference in rodents
authors: Mash, Deborah C.; Ameer, Barbara; Prou, Delphine; Howes, John F.; Maillet, Emeline L.
key_findings: Oral noribogaine reduced morphine withdrawal 88% (ED50=13mg/kg), brain/blood ratio 7±1, no conditioned place preference. Metabolite mediates ibogaine's anti-withdrawal effects.

title: Ibogaine-associated cardiac arrest and death: case report and review of the literature
authors: Meisner, Jessica A.; Wilcox, Susan R.; Richards, Jeremy B.
key_findings: Fatal case: 40yo used 4g ibogaine + 2g 'booster' for heroin detox. Found in asystole 8h later. Brain death from cardiac arrest. Reviews literature on ibogaine cardiotoxicity. Warns of unregulated internet sourcing.
contraindications: Pre-existing cardiac conditions; Liver enzyme abnormalities; Unregulated/unsupervised administration; Unknown dose or purity of ibogaine

title: Ibogaine for treating drug dependence. What is a safe dose?
authors: Schep, Leo J.; Slaughter, Robin J.; Galea, Sawitri; Newcombe, David
key_findings: Calculates theoretical safe oral dose of 0.87 mg/kg from animal NOAEL with safety factors; clinical doses of 6–30 mg/kg overlap toxic ranges; hERG IC50 values match therapeutic plasma concentrations
contraindications: Pre-existing cardiac conditions; CYP2D6 poor metaboliser status; Chronic sympathomimetic use (cocaine, methamphetamine); Medical comorbidities associated with long-term drug use
dosing_range: 0.87 mg/kg theoretical safe oral dose; clinical use 6–30 mg/kg oral
aliases: Schep 2016; Ibogaine Safe Dose Review; NOAEL Dose Calculation

title: Subjective effectiveness of ibogaine treatment for problematic opioid consumption: Short- and long-term outcomes and current psychological functioning
authors: Davis, Alan K.; Barsuglia, Joseph P.; Windham-Herman, Austin-Marley; Lynch, Marta; Polanco, Martin
key_findings: n=88 opioid patients (Crossroads, Mexico 2012-2015). 80% reported withdrawal elimination/drastic reduction. 30% never used opioids again; 54% of abstainers sustained ≥1yr. 41% sustained abstinence ≥6mo at survey.
contraindications: Prolonged QTc interval; History of heart disease; Pulmonary embolism; Deep vein thrombosis; Severe respiratory conditions (emphysema, COPD); Obesity; Gastrointestinal disorders (Crohn's, IBS); Chronic infectious diseases; Cerebellar dysfunction; Delirium; Organic brain disease or history of severe TBI; Epilepsy; Current pregnancy; Abnormal electrolytes; Impaired hepatic or renal function; Psychotic spectrum disorders; Bipolar I disorder; Current eating disorders; Alcohol/amphetamines/cocaine use within 1 week; Long-acting opioids (buprenorphine, methadone) within 4 weeks
aliases: Davis 2017; Ibogaine Opioid Outcomes Crossroads; Davis2017_Opioid_Outcomes

title: The antiaddictive effects of ibogaine: A systematic literature review of human studies
authors: dos Santos, Rafael G.; Bouso, José Carlos; Hallak, Jaime E. C.
key_findings: Systematic review of 8 human studies: 7 open-label case series suggest single ibogaine dose reduces opioid withdrawal within 24h lasting weeks-months; 1 noribogaine RCT (n=27) found no significant effect.
contraindications: Pre-existing cardiovascular disease; Concomitant opiate/opioid use during or immediately after ibogaine; Use of drugs of unknown purity in uncontrolled settings
aliases: Dos Santos 2017; Ibogaine Systematic Review Human Studies

title: Examination of the Phenomenology of the Ibogaine Treatment Experience: Role of Altered States of Consciousness and Psychedelic Experiences
authors: Heink, Annamarie; Katsikas, Steve; Lange-Altman, Tiffany
key_findings: 5d-ASC scores higher than psilocybin across all dimensions; VRS and G-ASC correlated with perceived change; 92% reported craving reduction at 4 weeks; ASCs related to self-reported change

title: Toxicokinetics of ibogaine and noribogaine in a patient with prolonged multiple cardiac arrhythmias after ingestion of internet purchased ibogaine
authors: Henstra, Marieke; Wong, Liza; Chahbouni, Abdel; Swart, Noortje; Allaart, Cor; Sombogaard, Ferdi
key_findings: Noribogaine, not ibogaine, drives prolonged cardiac toxicity. QTc 647 ms with TdP. Noribogaine t½ elimination 2540 h vs ibogaine 228 h. k₁₂/k₂₁ ratio 21.5 for noribogaine implies deep tissue sequestration.
contraindications: Pre-existing cardiac conditions; Concurrent CYP2D6-metabolised medications; Electrolyte imbalances (hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia)
dosing_range: 1400 mg oral (repeated doses over 12 hours, internet-sourced capsules)
aliases: Henstra 2017; Ibogaine Noribogaine Toxicokinetics Cardiac Arrhythmias

title: The Iboga Alkaloids
authors: Lavaud, Catherine; Massiot, Georges
tags: topic/mechanism; topic/analogues; topic/noribogaine; topic/18-mc; topic/receptor; topic/toxicity; topic/pharmacokinetics; method/review
key_findings: 117 new iboga alkaloids (2000–2016) from 14 species; ibogaine is weak MOR antagonist not agonist; 18-MC selectively targets α3β4 nAChR with no SERT/NMDA affinity; no scalable ibogaine synthesis exists
journal: Progress in the Chemistry of Organic Natural Products

title: Ibogaine treatment outcomes for opioid dependence from a twelve-month follow-up observational study
authors: Noller, Geoffrey E.; Frampton, Chris M.; Yazar-Klosinski, Berra
tags: topic/opioid; topic/efficacy; topic/withdrawal; topic/assessment; topic/policy; method/observational
key_findings: Single ibogaine treatment (mean 31.4 mg/kg) reduced ASI drug use score 81% at 12mo (p=0.002, n=8). BDI-II depression scores fell from 22.1 to 4.4 (p<0.001). 75% negative urine at 12mo. 1 fatality during treatment.
contraindications: Pre-existing cardiovascular conditions; CYP2D6 poor metaboliser status; Hepatic impairment; Concomitant CYP2D6 inhibitors
journal: The American Journal of Drug and Alcohol Abuse
aliases: Noller 2017; Noller 2016; NZ Ibogaine 12-Month Follow-Up; Noller2017_Ibogaine_12_Month_Outcomes

title: Anti-addiction Drug Ibogaine Prolongs the Action Potential in Human Induced Pluripotent Stem Cell-Derived Cardiomyocytes
authors: Rubi, Lena; Eckert, Daniel; Boehm, Stefan; Hilber, Karlheinz; Koenig, Xaver
key_findings: First proof ibogaine/noribogaine prolong action potential in human cardiomyocytes (hiPS-CM). Therapeutic concentrations retard repolarisation. Noribogaine's long half-life explains delayed cardiac events days after ingestion.

title: Detoxification from methadone using low, repeated, and increasing doses of ibogaine: A case report
authors: Wilkins, Clare; dos Santos, Rafael G.; Solá, Jordi; Aixalá, Marc; Cura, Pep; Moreno, Estefanía; Alcázar-Córcoles, Miguel Ángel; Hallak, Jaime E. C.; Bouso, José Carlos
key_findings: PROTOCOL INNOVATION: First documented low, multiple, ascending dose approach (150-600mg) alternating with tapering methadone. 47yo female, 17yr MMT successfully detoxified. No serious AEs, QTc within limits.
contraindications: QTc prolongation risk even at low doses — medical supervision required; Bradycardia risk — HR dropped from 85 to 53 bpm at 400-500 mg doses
dosing_range: 5 ascending doses (150, 300, 400, 500, 600 mg oral) alternating with halving methadone doses over 6 weeks

title: Phytochemical characterization of Tabernanthe iboga root bark and its effects on dysfunctional metabolism and cognitive performance in high-fat-fed C57BL/6J mice
authors: Bading-Taika, Bayissi; Akinyeke, Teemu; Magana, Armando A.; Choi, Jaewoo; Stevens, Jan F.
key_findings: T.iboga root bark phytochemistry + metabolic/cognitive effects in high-fat diet mice. IPHAMETRA Gabon + Oregon State collaboration. Characterises alkaloid profile and metabolic effects.
dosing_range: T. iboga root bark extract (mouse, oral gavage)

title: A case report SPECT study and theoretical rationale for the sequential administration of ibogaine and 5-MeO-DMT in the treatment of alcohol use disorder
authors: Barsuglia, Joseph P.; Polanco, Martin; Palmer, Robert; Malcolm, Benjamin J.; Kelmendi, Benjamin; Calvey, Tanya
key_findings: SPECT case: Sequential ibogaine (17.9 mg/kg) + 5-MeO-DMT in veteran with AUD. Increased perfusion bilateral caudate/putamen/insula/cerebellum post-treatment. Alcohol cessation at 1 mo, partial return at 2 mo.
dosing_range: 17.9 mg/kg ibogaine HCl (1550 mg total, split dose); 50 mg bufotoxin (~5–7 mg 5-MeO-DMT, vaporised)
aliases: Barsuglia 2018; Barsuglia SPECT 2018; Barsuglia Ibogaine 5-MeO-DMT SPECT

title: Treatment of Opioid Use Disorder with Ibogaine: Detoxification and Drug Use Outcomes
authors: Brown, Thomas Kingsley; Alper, Kenneth
tags: topic/opioid; topic/efficacy; topic/harm-reduction; topic/adverse-event; topic/cardiac; method/observational
key_findings: Prospective observational study (N=30): ibogaine reduced SOWS from 31.0 to 14.0 (p<.001); 50% opioid-free at 1 month; ASIC Drug Use improved at all time points to 12 months; methadone exposure predicted worse outcome.
journal: The American Journal of Drug and Alcohol Abuse
aliases: Brown 2018; Brown & Alper 2018; First prospective ibogaine OUD outcomes study; AJDAA ibogaine detoxification study; Brown2018_OUD_Ibogaine

title: A phenomenological investigation into the lived experience of ibogaine and its potential to treat opioid use disorders
authors: Camlin, Thaddeus James; Eulert, Donald; Horvath, Arthur Thomas; Bucky, Steven F.; Barsuglia, Joseph P.; Polanco, Martin
key_findings: 10 opioid-dependent participants reported ibogaine attenuated withdrawal (100%), eliminated cravings (100%), increased self-acceptance (100%), and induced spiritual outlook (70%)
contraindications: Preexisting medical conditions; Opioid use within 72 hours of ibogaine ingestion
dosing_range: 15–20 mg/kg therapeutic dose referenced; LD50 264 mg/kg

title: Ibogaine as a Treatment for Substance Misuse: Potential Benefits and Practical Dangers
authors: Corkery, John Martin
tags: topic/cardiac; topic/adverse-event; topic/toxicity; topic/opioid; topic/pharmacokinetics; topic/cyp2d6; topic/18-mc; topic/harm-reduction; method/review
